title: Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index
subtitle: Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by [V-Dem](#dod:v-dem), based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).
caption:
Share of the population in poverty vs. Electoral Democracy Index

Poverty is defined as living on less than $10 a day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries. The electoral democracy index is by V-Dem, based on expert estimates, and ranges from 0 to 1 (most democratic).

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of the population living in poverty and the Electoral Democracy Index for countries in 2025. The vertical axis measures the percentage of people living on less than $10 a day, expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices, while the horizontal axis displays the democracy score from 0 to 1. Points are colored by region, including North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data shows a negative relationship where higher democracy scores coincide with lower poverty rates. Countries with the lowest democracy scores, such as Laos, Tajikistan, and Egypt, cluster on the left side of the chart with poverty shares ranging from roughly 50% to over 80%. Burundi and Ethiopia also appear in this low-democracy, high-poverty quadrant. In the middle range, nations like Indonesia, Georgia, and Armenia show moderate democracy scores between 0.4 and 0.6, paired with poverty rates between 60% and 70%. Moving toward the right side of the chart, countries with higher democracy indices generally display lower poverty levels. Moldova, Brazil, and Costa Rica sit in the 0.7 to 0.9 range with poverty shares dropping to between 15% and 30%. The highest democracy scores, approaching 1.0, correspond to the lowest poverty rates, visible in countries like Australia, Chile, and several European nations where the share of the population in poverty falls below 10%. The distribution forms a downward slope from the top-left to the bottom-right, with the most democratic nations consistently occupying the lowest poverty bands.
